summary: Jeff Atwood examines how YouTube solved the longstanding problem of video codec incompatibility by adopting Flash Video as a universal standard. He explains that playing video on computers has historically been frustrating due to the need for specific codecs, with dozens of variants across different formats. YouTube and Google Video chose Flash because it 'just works' across browsers and platforms with minimal friction. While acknowledging that Flash Video quality is significantly inferior to alternatives like QuickTime or Windows Media, Atwood argues that universal compatibility trumps quality for most web video use cases. He concludes that Flash Video has become the de facto internet video standard, exemplifying the 'worse is better' philosophy.
key_points: Video codec fragmentation created 'codec hell' where users needed specific codecs installed to play different video formats
YouTube chose Flash Video because it works universally across browsers and platforms with easy installation
Flash Video quality is dramatically worse than alternatives like QuickTime or Windows Media
For most web video content, universal accessibility matters more than video quality
The 'worse is better' principle explains why an inferior technology can win through simplicity and ubiquity
money_quotes: Playing video on a computer has always been a crapshoot.
As the old saying goes, we love standards: that's why we have so many of them.
That's why we call it codec hell.
YouTube elected to cut the Gordian knot of video codecs: they chose Flash Video, which 'just works' on most computers.
Flash Video quality is, in a word, hideous.
Quality is largely irrelevant for most video content on the web.
It'll never win any quality awards, but it's still recognizable as video. Therefore it wins by default.
Flash Video is the new internet video standard. Sometimes worse really is better.
key_insight: Universal compatibility and ease of use can trump technical quality, as YouTube proved by choosing the inferior but ubiquitous Flash Video format over superior but fragmented alternatives.
